items.question: What does a support vector machine maximize in the hard-margin linear case?
items.explanation: Hard-margin SVM minimizes ||w||² subject to yᵢ(wᵀxᵢ + b) ≥ 1, equivalent to maximizing the minimum geometric margin 2/||w||₂ between parallel margin hyperplanes.

items.question: In soft-margin SVM, increasing C generally:
options: Widens the margin and tolerates more errors
Narrows the margin and penalizes violations more heavily
Converts the model to logistic regression
items.answer: Narrows the margin and penalizes violations more heavily
items.explanation: Large C increases the weight on Σξᵢ in ||w||² + C Σξᵢ — the optimizer allows less slack, producing a tighter fit to training labels.

items.question: Which training points typically become support vectors?
options: Only points far from the boundary
Points on or inside the margin (and violators in soft margin)
items.answer: Points on or inside the margin (and violators in soft margin)
items.explanation: Support vectors have non-zero αᵢ — they lie on the margin boundary y(wᵀx+b)=1 or, with soft margin, inside/violating the margin when slack is active.

items.question: The linear SVM decision function for a new point x is:
options: sign(wᵀx + b) via score f(x) = wᵀx + b
items.answer: sign(wᵀx + b) via score f(x) = wᵀx + b
items.explanation: Linear SVM predicts class from the sign of the margin score wᵀx + b — not a sigmoid probability unless separately calibrated.

items.question: Geometric margin of a point (xᵢ, yᵢ) with yᵢ ∈ {−1,+1} is:
items.explanation: Functional margin is yᵢ(wᵀxᵢ + b); dividing by L2 norm ||w||₂ gives perpendicular distance to the hyperplane — the geometric margin.

items.question: Compared to logistic regression, SVM decision_function scores are best used for:
items.explanation: SVM scores rank separation quality; ROC & AUC consume these ranks. Calibrated probabilities require Platt scaling or a probabilistic model like logistic regression.
